speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape number 4, August 8, 1996, interview of Yehoshua Trigor. Your were active in the cultural life of the Vilna ghetto. You were a member-- you had mentioned you were a member of the theater groups and the choir groups. Could you describe some of the activities you took part in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the theater-- the Hebrew theater put on a performance called [NON-ENGLISH], by Pinski. We did not have the book, but the teacher Dimentman reconstructed it from memory. And I played a small part in it. The-- the Hebrew choir was headed by a conductor called Dumarshkin. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had sopranos, alts, tenors, and was baritones. We sang like [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 'Herbstlied' by Mendelssohn and song of autumn-- 'Autumn Song' by Mendelssohn. We sang the 'Grand March' of And we song-- we sang-- we were singing also many Hebrew Zionist songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did you rehearse with these groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once or twice a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did this have public support from the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: It did, because I assume their line was that as long as the Judenrat exists and as long as people work and don't run away and don't go into the forests, then they should have some release, some entertainment. And this was the entertainment. And I think that the-- the Germans knew about it as well. This was contrary to let's say the Kovno ghetto, where the leadership helped the underground. In Vilna ghetto, I don't think it was like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time that you were-- for the year you were in the ghetto, were there any Jews killed during this period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were from time to time Jews killed, one or two, for various so-called offenses. But by and large it was the quiet period. And anyway, I was concentrating on trying to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a weapon. I was about to go out with Dr. Leon Bernstein and his group. But in the meantime, his group fell apart. I will not go into details. And he himself joined the FBO. In the meantime, I had to leave the ghetto quite abruptly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because the man who sold me the weapon was caught. And I was tipped off by Chaim Trapido, the director of the [NON-ENGLISH] in Vilna. It was a Kovno newspaper. And I was tipped off and they told me I have no time to go to my room to take the weapon, so a friend of mine and I went to a place where Professor Stakauskas sent us, because he apparently had some information that something is going to happen in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. We're going to come back to leaving of the ghetto. I just want to pose a few more questions to examine the time that you were in the ghetto. Did you know about Ponary?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew, yes. But I have never been there at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what did you know of that location?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew because the-- the FBO and others said that people are not taking to any other working places. They are taken to Ponary and being shot. And we knew also about the Ninth Fort in Kovno, because there was contact between the ghettos.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you know about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that Jews were killed. They were shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in Kovno.

speaker: interviewee
text: Both in Kovno and in Vilna, in Vilna and Ponary and Kovno in the Ninth Fort.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. Did you have any contact with Jacob Gens while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I may have met him once, but I had no direct contact. I met his-- I knew his daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ada. She was half-Jewish, because her mother was a Lithuanian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Jacob Gens' position in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the head of the ghetto, was ghetto chef.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your opinion of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my opinion, which I kept to myself, was that he was a very tragic figure, that he's trying to save some Jews at the expense of others. I wasn't quite sure whether morally or according to halakah, to Jewish law, one should assume that position. But of course, it was easy for me to say. And this may also be practically hindsight, like a Monday morning quarterback. It's difficult to judge Gens. Gens' intentions were good. Dessler I believe, his assistant, the police chief for Vilna, I believe was already unwillingly-- he became a Gestapo agent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that name for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dessler?

speaker: interviewer
text: Dessler.

speaker: interviewee
text: D-E-S-S-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was the police chief. And you think that he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Unwillingly, he became a collaborator of-- Gens actually was shot by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But I know he was shot. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I was in the ghetto at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- we walked out of the ghetto. We took off our jackets. It was a hot summer day. It was also the 13th of July, because everything happened on the same day. And we walked out and we went south. And after about 10 days of many eventful things that happened, we were shot at and somebody tried to snatch my shoes, my boots. And my boots had all-- not only the boots, but also my money. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you wounded?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was injured, yes, in my foot. And then afterwards in Romania I had to-- I jumped a train when Russians tried to check my documents and they were not very happy with them. I jumped and I injured my nose.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll come to that event when we look at your post-war activities. When you left the ghetto with your friend--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --it was after-- after you were warned. What was the status of the FPO when you left in July of 1943.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- they were around these days, I don't remember exactly, that they were-- there was a question of Wittenberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was Wittenberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wittenberg was the-- the commander of the FPO, who was-- whose name was given away by the Lithuanian Communists in town that were captured by the Nazis, something that I wasn't surprised at all, knowing the nature of the Lithuanian approach to Communists and to Russians the last 500 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was, of course, a question discussed then and discussed in independent Israel afterwards whether the FPO was entitled to-- to sacrifice its commander and whether he accepted it willingly or whether he was forced. Why do you use the word sacrificed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to dwell too much on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But just one point. Why do you use the word the FPO was-- was right to sacrifice their commander. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because that's what the Germans said. They either give us Wittenberg or we'll destroy the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that time the--

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point--

speaker: interviewee
text: -- FPO declared mobilization of its members. They wants freed Wittenberg from the hands of the Jewish police that wanted to give him to Germans. The Germans came back and threatened to destroy the ghetto. At that time Gens and Dessler and the Jewish ghetto leadership [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] called for a meeting of many people and said, look, you all want to be destroyed because of one man? Help us to find leader-- Wittenberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you at that meeting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now.

speaker: interviewer
text: But who told you about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I-- I even don't know whether this is information that I knew at the time. I knew some. I saw some people with-- with weapons and I saw-- but, oh, it was later that I read about it. And you must understand that two of my close friends are both Holocaust researchers.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. And this surrender of Wittenberg, about what month in '43 would this have occurred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been in July or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before you left.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This you can check the details.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you familiar-- did you know Josef Glazman?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know-- I knew Josef Glazman. He was the [NON-ENGLISH] of Betar in Lithuania. And he was in Vilna in the police. But of course, he was working with the underground, with the FPO.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was. OK. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he was killed when they went to-- to the woods. But on the way, I believe there were-- they were surprised by a German unit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you've mentioned that you knew Abner Kovner while you were in the ghetto, but that you would not want to comment on him.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes. I don't want to comment on-- on him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine. How well did you know Dr. Leo Bernstein?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew him very well. I knew him from home. I knew his sister, Miriam. I knew them from [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hope you were going to be able to do with Dr. Bernstein and his group?

speaker: interviewee
text: To go to the woods to survive, to outlive Hitler, and to go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Before we look at your time at-- after you leave the ghetto on July 13th, was it hard or easy to get this gun and ammunition that you had in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course it was difficult. It was dangerous. I nearly lost my life, because if I didn't get out-- after I left, the ghetto administration published that I was caught and-- and killed by the Germans. So when I came back in 1944, some people thought that I came back from the dead. They said, what are you doing here? That's what they said. I don't know what they published about me, but I'm here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you left the ghetto with your friend, you had a note from a professor at Vilna University.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Professor, yes, Stakauskas. He was a priest and a professor of bibliography.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what did this note say?

speaker: interviewee
text: It says [NON-ENGLISH]. It is advisable quickly to change your living quarters. [NON-ENGLISH]. If you will have some difficulties-- [NON-ENGLISH] and this address. If there will be some difficulties, please contact the priest Gediminas Peletskis And they gave the address. I forgot the address. I tried when I was after the war in '91 or '92 in Vilna to get his address and his details from the curia. But they did not have the documents, because the Russians have confiscated in the '80s all the archives of the-- of the bishop in Vilna, probably in all of Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Let's go back to your contact with this priest. How long did it get-- take for you and your friend to find this priest?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were separated-- the shooting, my friend and I, and we-- we-- we got there separately. And I didn't know all the time about my friend that he was alive, because the priest, although he was not trained in intelligence, knew very well how to separate things, was very smart. And I didn't know until just before the German rule fell apart and we were about to be liberated and we could hear the guns of the front coming close. We-- I didn't even know that he existed. And he didn't know about me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your friend who you left the ghetto with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Professor 'Zirnofsky,' Zirnauskas, professor of chemistry, very talented. He did not want to go to Palestine or to Israel. He died a few years ago. He was a professor of chemistry at the Vilnius University.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the dean of, actually, chemistry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell his name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zirnauskas, Z-I-R-N-A-U-S-K-A-S. I don't remember his first name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, where did the priest send you after you made contact with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He sent me to-- to one farmer. And afterwards, either the farmer was not happy to keep me, maybe he did not believe in my flimsy excuse, you know, that I was a Lithuanian from the area of Telz, I said, not from my hometown, and I was running away from the Germans. Then alternatively I said I was looking for my parents that were expelled by the Russians. And I was looking-- I heard that they jumped the train in this area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after that, he put me to another farmer. The other farmer was called Jakimavicius. And would you spell the name of that farmer?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-A-K-I-M-A-V-I-C-I-U-S. And there, after a while, the priest, when he visited me and discovered that I knew plenty about the Catholic religion, because sometimes I used to remain in class when they had religion classes. I didn't have to go to the Jewish religion classes, because I was far ahead of-- of my teacher. That was the rabbi's daughter. And I was more ahead because I-- for eight years, I had a permanent rabbi training me every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I used to stay. And I knew about the catechism. I knew other things, so I used to teach catechism to daughters of Jakimavicius. And he was a Lithuanian and I taught them in Lithuanian. What-- what-- But

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried not to appear publicly and tried to keep to myself. And I understood that in case of a really big inquiry would be a problem. And there was, of course, some doubt about my being a Lithuanian Catholic. And this is when the story with the pig came and actually helped me to establish myself. I was invited to sviezines. That means fresh, fresh blood. And you do it by stabbing a pig and drinking its hot, gushing blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell the name of that ceremony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sviezines. S-V-I-E-Z-I-N-E-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a hunch why I was invited, because normally, you give such an honor to an uncle or a father or-- or an important person or the priest. I was given it because some people said, lets do it, because the priest says that he's related to the bishop and he's a refugee and we should help him, but he kind of has sad eyes. He looks like a Jew. But he doesn't speak like a Jew, said. They said, OK, so we don't know. So let's give him this test. So they gave me the test. And the same morning I prayed in Hebrew secretly. And then I went to the party. And of course, I-- I took a big knife and I hit the pig right into the throat, where I was supposed to, because I've seen the ceremony in Lithuania. I didn't have to drink the blood before the war, because everybody knew I was Jewish. This time, I drank the blood would great gusto. And I don't know how I swallowed it. And everybody else joined in. And on the way back, when everybody was drunk from locally made liquor, my host said that-- don't feel so, so big. It's not because you are a teacher or because you are so smart. Simply they wanted to use this as a test to see if you are Jewish or not. And thank god you're not.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this period of time, did you have false papers while you were on the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had false papers from the priest Gediminas Peletskis. But of course, these were not-- these were papers of a village priest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name on those papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe Aleksas Tautkus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-L-E-K-S-A-S Tautkus, T-A-U-T-K-U-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides this episode with the killing of the pig, while you were working on the farm, was there ever another time when you thought your life was in danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, whenever Germans used to approach the-- the farm, I used to-- from the back, I always-- I had my place prepared. And I used to hear a motor, because no-- none of the local people had a motor car. So if it was a motor car it must have been either police or army.

speaker: interviewee
text: So whenever I heard the-- and we were quite removed from the road. I could easily sneak out into the woods, because it was a wooded area. And I had a place prepared two, three miles away. When everything quietened down, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they left, I used to come back. And the farmer's wife once asked me, why did you go away? I mean, are you OK? I said, no. You see, the Germans might draft me into the-- into work or into the Lithuanian battalions. I don't want to go to the front.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't trust the Germans more than I trust the Russians. I'm a Lithuanian nationalist. And the Russians didn't-- didn't honor our freedom, neither did the Germans. They did not keep their word that they're going to restore independent Lithuania. Lithuania became another district of the eastern provinces.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time that you were living on these false papers, what did you know about the progress of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew more or less the progress of the war, sometimes from the priest and sometimes from other people that were connected with the Armia Krajowa. Armia Krajowa was a Polish underground. There were two, Armia Krajowa and Armia Ludowa. The Armia Krajowa was anti-Semitic and often killed Jews. In yesterday's there was an article that you should-- I mean, one should read about what they did after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Armia Krajowa had contacts with London. The Polish government in exile was in London. And they used to have radio messages, so they knew. And some of if used to-- to drip to the local population. So we knew a little. During this period of time that you were on these false papers, what did you do to keep up your spirits?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, praying, singing quietly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Israeli songs, Hebrew songs that I knew, and thinking and planning my life, what I will do in Palestine after Hitler is defeated and I run away from the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you hope to do in Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, strange enough, I hoped to do exactly what I did, because I-- I wanted to work in the foreign ministry. And that's where I ended up in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. When and by whom were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was liberated on the 13th of July, 1944 by the Soviet army. The whole front of the Russians-- of the Germans collapsed. And all of a sudden-- all of a sudden, we found ourselves free. At that time, since there were many Lithuanians and many units of partisans still on the loose, of Lithuanian partisans, the priest said, don't remain here. It's dangerous. Go back to a big town. I said, I-- I was in Vilna. He said, go back to Vilna. A day before that introduced me again to my friend 'Zirnofsky,' and we went together back to Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And what-- what-- what type of conditions for Lithuanian Jews did you find when you got back to Lithuania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I found very few Jews. And everybody was looking for somebody. And I was looking for an apartment. I don't think my apartment was in existence, so I was free.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I found an empty house on Gedimino, on the nicest street. And I made some changes, you know, a new lock and a few things. I found some artisans and I got together a little furniture. And I lived there and-- What did you do to support yourself in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, I still had a little money from my boots, the golden coins. I had a few. And then I started immediate to work. And it was a Jewish community and I started to work for the Soviet government that re-established itself. And I worked in the ministry of light industry. | used my work there to help Polish Jews-- I mean, Vilna Jews. Vilna before 1939 belonged to Poland, so these people wanted to leave the Soviet Union, so they wanted to repatriate to Poland. And for that they needed to document that they were not essential, their work was not essential for-- for the Ministry, so I gave them such documents. The last document I gave was to Mrs. Yoffe and to her little girl, who is now married in-- somewhere in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Would you spell the name of the person who you gave the last document to, Mrs.--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mrs. Yoffe, Y-O-F-F-E, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] her daughter Esti.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe-- I believe it was Esti, E-S-T-I. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She worked-- they both worked for me, for us, for the Ministry. And I gave them the document. And then I disappeared myself. And in three days, we all were on a truck that we rented. And they took us not on the highway, but on byways, to avoid-- to avoid the border, because although Poland-- the free Poland was, so to speak, a-- still under the Soviets, but it was different from the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. What date are we talking about when you left Vilna with these others in the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess in-- in July.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So you were in Vilna when the--

speaker: interviewee
text: One year.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when the Germans surrendered.

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- when the Germans--

speaker: interviewer
text: In May, in May of '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of reactions in Vilna to the news of the surrender?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody was happy that the Germans lost the war. But I-- I frankly was more worried to get out. What-- because Lithuanian Jews were in a predicament. They couldn't get out like others, like Polish refugees Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or just from Vilna, because we were made Soviet citizens again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you decide in July '45 to leave by truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I decided the moment I came, in July '43-- '44, excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --when I--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. What I meant was, you actually left in July of '45, and where did you go from Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did I go? I went to Bialystock, B-I-A-L-Y-S-T-O-K. And from Bialystok, we went to Lodz, L-O-D-Z. And from there, we had already contact with the Berihah, with the-- with the Jewish underground that originated in Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how did they help you get to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they helped us. They simply started to take us in groups. Our group was taken via Czechoslovakia, Hungary, to Romania, in the hope that-- to set sail from one of the Black Sea ports. What happened was that the Russians prevented any ships from leaving. And then they decided that we'll try Italy. So they brought us from Romania via Austria from the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Russian part into the British part, under very clever tricks--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that the Jewish Brigade group--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what they did?

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us in huge trucks of the Jewish Brigade group. And when-- they had false papers and-- that send them there. And nobody saw us inside the trucks. And when the British military police stops stopped them and asked them, what-- what is their mission, they said, this is a very secret mission. It's TTG. So the British asked, what is TTG? They say, [NON-ENGLISH], which means, [INAUDIBLE]. TGB is in Arabic the same thing, Geschaften means business. And the-- the British didn't know. And we-- we crossed through in Tarvisio. We went to Mestre in Venice. And then we felt really free for the first time. And I remember we went to Mestre to the entertainment park. And we rant those little motor cars, you know, electric cars that collide with one another, like children, you know. And after that we were in Italy until the Berihah told us one day to go again using the British army Palestine units, Jewish units, trucks. We went to Monopoli in southern Italy. And we left for Palestine, which we reached before Rosh Hashanah, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
